elements.sent-2411-004: China 215006}\, \begin{center \begin{minipage {130mm {\bf Abstract \;\; This paper explores the optimal investment problem of a renewal risk model with generalized Erlang distributed interarrival times.}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: We assume that the phases of the interarrival time can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-006: The price of the risky asset is driven by the CEV model and the insurer aims to maximize the exponential utility of the terminal wealth by asset allocation.
elements.sent-2411-007: By solving the corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, when the interest rate is zero, the concavity of the solution as well as the the explicit expression of the investment policy is shown.
elements.sent-2411-008: When the interest rate is not zero, the explicit expression of the optimal investment strategy is shown, the structure as well as the concavity of the value function is proved.
elements.sent-2411-009: The optimal investment problem of a general insurer has been studied under various settings since the work of \cite{Hipp2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: With the exponential utility, \cite{Yang2005 consider the optimal investment strategy for an insurer with jump-diffusion surplus process when the risky asset follow a Geometric Brownian motion in which the closed form of the optimal investment strategy is shown.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The optimal investment for an insurer with cointegrated assets with CRRA utility was studied in \cite{Chiu2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: All above mentioned optimization problems are investigated in the Markovian framework.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the compound Poisson model, the interclaim times are exponential distributed.
elements.sent-2411-014: But exponential distributed interclaim times have no memory about the time elapsed since the last claim.
elements.sent-2411-015: To overcome such drawback, probabilist brought out the renewal process to characterize the surplus of the insurance company.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, for the Erlang(n) interclaim times, \cite{Albre2005 and \cite{Albrecher2006 calculate the moment-generating function of the discounted dividends of horizontal barrier strategies and show that in general horizontal barrier strategies is not the optimal dividend strategy.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Later, \cite{Bai2017,Bai2021 investigate the optimal investment and dividend problem of the Sparre Andersen model in the framework of viscosity solution.}
elements.sent-2411-018: One can refer \cite{Bergel2015,Li2006 for more control studies about renewal surplus process.}
elements.sent-2411-019: As far as we know, the explicit solution of optimal utility of renewal process is not easy to find, thus, the paper explores the optimal investment problem of Erlang(n) distributed interclaim renewal process.
elements.sent-2411-020: Now we describe the renewal claim process formally.
elements.sent-2411-021: Let $\{J_t\}$ be a homogenous Markov chain on the state space $\{1,2,\cdots, n\}$ with an intensity matrix of the form \begin{equation}\left( {ccccc} -\lambda_1 \lambda_1 0 \cdots 0 0 -\lambda_2 \lambda_2 \cdots 0 \vdots \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots \lambda_n 0 0 \cdots -\lambda_n  \right),\end{equation} i.e., the process moves through $1\rightarrow 2 \rightarrow\cdots\rightarrow n\rightarrow 1\rightarrow \cdots$ and stays in state $i\in\{1,2,\cdots, n\}$ for an exponential time with parameter $\lambda_i$ --- this is often referred to as an exponential clock.
elements.sent-2411-022: When the exponential clock rings, $J_t$ jumps to the next state.
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, when the current state is $n$, after staying an exponential time with parameter $\lambda_n$, $J_t$ will jump to state $1$ and a claim occurs.
elements.sent-2411-024: We also assume that the phases of $J_t$ can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-025: It is obvious that if $n=1$, then the surplus process degenerates to the compound Poisson process.
elements.sent-2411-026: As the Markov chain $J_t$ has $n$ states, the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation forms a system of $n$-dimensional coupled equation.
elements.sent-2411-027: We divide the optimization problem into two cases: the first where the interest rate is zero, and the second where the interest rate is non-zero.
elements.sent-2411-028: The solutions to the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation in these two cases take different mathematical forms.
elements.sent-2411-029: The main challenge lies in demonstrating that the solution to each HJB equation is concave.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the first case, when the interest rate is zero, we prove the concavity of the solution using the Laplace transform of the Markov chain with killing.
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, we derive the explicit expressions for both the value function and the optimal investment strategy.
elements.sent-2411-032: In the second case, where the interest rate is non-zero, it is more difficult to express the solution to the HJB equation explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, we establish the existence of a solution, and rigorously prove the explicit form and concavity of the value function by decoupling the system of simultaneous equations and applying the Banach fixed point theorem.
elements.sent-2411-034: One might compare the Erlang(n) renewal model with the regime-switching model where various optimization problems have been addressed within the regime-switching framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: For example, \cite{Elliott2011 explores an optimal investment problem of an insurance company aiming to maximize the minimal expected exponential utility with regime switching.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Later, \cite{Liu2014 investigates an optimal investment problem of an insurer with risk constraint and regime-switching.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Other optimization problems in this area include \cite{Jiang2012 , which explores the optimal dividend problem for an insurance company in the presence of regime shifts, and \cite{zou2014 , which studies optimal consumption, investment, and insurance policies under regime switching.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: A key distinction between our model and the regime-switching is: in the regime-switching model, external regime influence variables such as the interest rate, claim intensity, return rate, and volatility rate and so on.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, regime changes typically do not lead to a lump sum reduction in wealth.
elements.sent-2411-040: In contrast, in our Erlang($n$) model, at the end of phase $n$, not only the Markov chain enters a new phase (phase 1), but also a claim occurs, resulting in a lump sum decrease in wealth.
elements.sent-2411-041: Given these differences, we focus on the optimal investment problem in the context of Erlang($n$) interclaim times.
elements.sent-2411-042: The main contributions of this work are twofold: 1.
elements.sent-2411-043: The optimal investment problems for the classical compound Poisson claim process are well-established.
elements.sent-2411-044: The Erlang-distributed interclaim model extends the classical compound Poisson model.
elements.sent-2411-045: The explicit solutions derived in our study provide valuable insights for other optimization problems involving renewal processes.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-046: To demonstrate the concavity of the solution to the HJB equation, we employ the Laplace transform of the Markov chain, decouple the system of simultaneous equations, and apply the Banach fixed point theorem, showcasing the novelty of the mathematical methodology.
elements.sent-2411-047: The structure of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-048: Section \ref{model1 introduces the surplus process of the insurance company.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The goal is to maximize the exponential utility of terminal wealth by investment.
elements.sent-2411-050: The problem is then divided into two cases.
elements.sent-2411-051: Section \ref{ere2121 studies the case where the interest rate is zero, presenting the explicit solution for both the optimal value function and the optimal strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In Section \ref{intere2 , we explore the case where the interest rate is non-zero.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In this section, we provide the explicit expression for the optimal investment policy and apply the decoupling equation technique, along with the Banach fixed point theorem, to demonstrate the concavity of the solution to the HJB equation.
elements.sent-2411-054: Section \ref{4738478374 analyzes the sensitivity of various parameters on the optimal policy and value function.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, Section \ref{conclusion concludes the paper, while some detailed proofs are provided in the Appendix.}

elements.sent-2411-057: Let $T>0$ be a finite time horizon and $\mathscr{F _t$ stands the information available before time $t\in[0,T].$ The surplus of the insurer follows \begin{equation}dC_t=cdt-d\sum_{i=1}^{N_t}Y_i\end{equation} where $c>0$ is the premium rate, $N_t$ is a renewal counting process representing the number of claims before time $t$, $\{Y_i\}_{i=1 ^{\infty}$ are independent and identically distributed positive random variables and $Y_i$ represents the size of the $i$-th claim.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The interclaim times are independent and follow a generalized Erlang(n) distribution.

elements.sent-2411-061: The insurer is allowed to invest in a financial market consisting two assets, one risk-free asset and one risky asset.
elements.sent-2411-062: The price process $S_0(t)$ of the risk-free asset follows \begin{equation}dS_0(t)=rS_0(t)dt,\end{equation} where $r>0$ is the risk-free interest rate.
elements.sent-2411-063: We assume that the price process of the risky asset is driven by the CEV model \begin{equation}dS(t)=S(t)(\mu dt+\sigma S(t)^\beta dW(t)),\end{equation} where $\mu>r$ is the expected instantaneous return rate of the risky asset; $k>0$ is a constant; $\beta\ge 0$ represents the elasticity parameter; $W(t)$ is a standard Brownian motion which is independent of the compound renewal process.
elements.sent-2411-064: When $\beta=0,$ a CEV model degenerates to a geometric Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-065: The investment strategy is denoted by $\{a_t\}_{0\le t\le T}$, where $a_t\in\mathbb{R}$ denotes the total amount of money invested in the risky asset.
elements.sent-2411-066: Under the strategy $a$, the surplus process of the insurance company follows \begin{equation}\label{sa1wqw} dX_t^a=(rX_t+(\mu-r)a_t+c)dt+\sigma S_t^\beta a_tdW_t-d\sum_{i=1}^{N_t}Y_i\end{equation} We call a strategy $\{a_t\}_{0\le t\le T}$ admissible if for any $t\in[0,T]$, $a_t$ is $\mathscr{F _t$ progressively measurable, $\mathbb{E [\int_0^{+\infty a_t^2S(t)^{2\beta dt]<+\infty$ and the equation \eqref{sa1wqw admits a unique strong solution.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-068: We consider the optimal investment problem which aims to maximize exponential utility of the terminal wealth, mathematically speaking, the utility function of the insurer is defined as \begin{equation}U(x)=-\frac{1}{m}e^{-mx },\end{equation} where $m>0$ is a constant.
elements.sent-2411-069: Such an utility function plays an important role in mathematical finance and actuarial science.
elements.sent-2411-070: The constant $m$ is called the absolute risk aversion parameter.
elements.sent-2411-071: For any investment strategy $\{a_t\}_{0\le t\le T}\in \mathscr{U _{ad}$ and any initial state $(s,x,i)$, define the utility of the strategy $a$ as \begin{equation}J(t,x,s,i;a)=E\left[U(X_T^a)|X_t=x,S_t=s, J_t=i\right].\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-198: Let $\{J_t\}$ be a homogenous Markov chain on the phase state space $\{1,2,\cdots, n\}$ with an intensity matrix of the form \begin{equation}\left( {ccccc} -\lambda_1 \lambda_1 0 \cdots 0 0 -\lambda_2 \lambda_2 \cdots 0 \vdots \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots \lambda_n 0 0 \cdots -\lambda_n  \right),\end{equation} i.e., the phase moves through $1\rightarrow 2 \rightarrow\cdots\rightarrow n\rightarrow 1\rightarrow \cdots$ and stays in phase $i\in\{1,2,\cdots, n\}$ for the intensity with parameter $\lambda_i$ which is also called an exponential clock.
elements.sent-2411-199: When the exponential clock rings, $J_t$ jumps to the next phase.
elements.sent-2411-200: Specifically, if the current phase is $n$, after waiting for an exponential time with parameter $\lambda_n$, $J_t$ will jump to phase $1$.
elements.sent-2411-201: We assume the claims only occurs when the phase jumps from $n$ to 1.
elements.sent-2411-202: If $n=1$, the renewal process degenerates to the compound Poisson process.
elements.sent-2411-203: We assume that the phases of $J_t$ can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-204: For example, the lithium battery-powered electric bicycles are common vehicle in China.
elements.sent-2411-205: Since unstable voltage can cause lithium batteries to explode or catch fire during charging, electric vehicles with lithium batteries are prohibited from being charged indoors.
elements.sent-2411-206: Each incident caused by the indoor charging electric bicycle generates extensive coverage across various media platforms, such as TV news, TikTok and X, thereby raising public awareness of such incidents.
elements.sent-2411-207: The government also issues a series of regulations and guidelines to avoid future possible accidents.
elements.sent-2411-208: At this point, insurance companies can infer from public opinion and human memory of the last disaster that they are in phase 1, where the probability of new claims arising from such accidents is virtually zero.
elements.sent-2411-209: As time passes, public concern diminishes, and platforms stop promoting awareness, though the government may continue to run advertisements on the safe charging requirement for lithium batteries.
elements.sent-2411-210: This marks the transition to phase 2.
elements.sent-2411-211: As time continues to pass, people's awareness fades, and advertisements expire, and the state turns to phase 3, where the insurance company will face more claim risk.

elements.sent-2411-224: The regime-switching model assume that the model parameters such as the interest rate, claim intensity, return rate, and volatility rate changes according to the current regime, while the regime switching typically do not lead to a direct reduction in wealth.
elements.sent-2411-225: In contrast, the Erlang($n$) model allows both changes in parameters and wealth upon the phase entering a new phase.
elements.sent-2411-226: Specifically, when the phase jumps from phase $n$ to phase 1, the intensity of interarrival time will turns to that of phase 1 and the wealth will reduce by the size of a claim.
elements.sent-2411-227: Our present article focus on the optimal investment problem in the context of Erlang($n$) interclaim times.
elements.sent-2411-228: The main contributions of this work are twofold: (1) We extend the classical result of optimal investment problems with compound Poisson claims to that of a renewal process with Erlang-distributed interarrival time.
